what_it_does: This file articulates a high-level UI design for projecting an underlying structured substrate state into interactive surfaces, defining principles, three primary UI surfaces, a visual grammar for incomplete structure, a sequence rail, a context resolver, work queues, AI representation, and invariants.
observations: The UI is state-first rather than screen-first: the visual surface changes because substrate state changes.
It proposes three primary surfaces: a structural map, a sequence rail, and a context resolver panel.
It states the user should always be able to find four things: where am I, what is known, what is not yet known, and what can happen next.

It defines a visual grammar for addressable incompletion, including known nodes, proto-positions, unknown nodes, unknown properties, open sockets, orphan relations, ghost targets, branch points, and ambiguities.
The sequence rail is described as an ordered projection of state progression, not merely a timeline.
Start and end boundaries are allowed to be vague or candidate; the UI should not force premature precision.
The context resolver panel uses interrogative axes: who, what, when, where, how, why, and what next.
Actions are generated from active channels and are registry-driven, not generic menus.
Work queues are projections of incomplete structure, not manually created task lists.
Saved queries can become reusable UI modes or state lenses.
AI is represented as a typed block, not a magical chat box.
Chat is treated as one sequence projection, not the fundamental storage container.
Voice memo extraction is offered as a practical first application of the sequence UI.
The UI explicitly supports partiality: rough ideas, unknowns, ambiguities, and deferred decisions are rendered as structure.
A minimal prototype is sketched with five areas: structural map, sequence rail, resolver panel, work queue panel, and event/state log.
A set of invariants closes the document, including that visible items must lift back to structure and chat must not become the storage primitive.
standouts: State-first UI: the screen is a projection of current substrate state, not a fixed workflow.
Incomplete structure is rendered as a valid state rather than hidden.
AI is represented as a typed block whose work appears as structure, not as an invisible chat master.
Work queues are query projections of incomplete structure, not user-managed task lists.
Chat is demoted from storage container to one possible projection.
conventions: Follows a design-document convention with front matter, title, authorship, numbered sections, and a closing invariants list.
Uses substrate-specific terminology consistently: address space, fibre, socket, ghost target, orphan relation, active channel, materialised node.
Breaks conventional UI metaphors by rejecting wizard, dashboard, file explorer, chat thread, and graph visualisation as the primary container.
concerns: The design remains highly abstract with no concrete data model, rendering technology, or implementation mechanism described.
Some terms are introduced without file-local definitions, risking circularity for readers unfamiliar with the broader substrate.
The minimal prototype is sketched at a layout level but lacks interaction details or state-transition rules.
The active-channel action registry is asserted but not specified in this file.
notes: A design spec proposing that the UI should project substrate state through a structural map, sequence rail, context resolver, and work-queue surfaces, with incomplete structure rendered as first-class visible state.
questions: What is the exact data model for the substrate that the UI projects?
How are active channels discovered and registered to generate context-specific actions?
How are start and end boundaries represented, validated, and allowed to remain vague?
What rendering technology or layout engine would support the structural map and sequence rail?
How does user input map to state transitions and events?
How are branches persisted, visualised, and resumed?
What distinguishes an AI block type from a tool or user block in concrete terms?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to describe what the sequence/progression face becomes as interface, and the file does that by laying out UI principles, surfaces, grammar, and invariants; purpose and content align.
apparent_themes: Addressable incompletion as visible, valid structure
Typed actor/block model including AI and tools
Support for partiality and deferred decisions
Substrate as the single source of truth, with chat as a derived view
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to define a new UI paradigm where interaction surfaces are direct projections of an underlying structured knowledge/progression substrate, so that users can navigate ideas, unknowns, and decisions visually rather than through conventional linear workflows or chat.
novelty: The treatment of UI as a projection of substrate state with first-class rendering of incomplete, unknown, ghost, and orphan structures; the idea that work queues are automatically generated from incomplete structure queries; and the deliberate demotion of chat from storage primitive to one of many projections.
contribution: A vocabulary and surface layout for a state-first UI, including the structural map, sequence rail, context resolver, work queue, and event log, plus a visual grammar for incomplete structure and invariants that keep the UI anchored to the substrate rather than to screens or chat history.
summary_for_embedding: A design document specifying a state-first UI that projects an underlying structured substrate through a structural map, sequence rail, context resolver, and work-queue surfaces, rendering incomplete and unknown structures as visible, actionable states.
intention_for_embedding: To define how the sequence/progression face of the substrate should be experienced and interacted with as a user interface.
